clean_story:
Fractured stories. Trust as glue.

A lie is a fake bridge—if the planks don’t match, it collapses under weight. The word "consistency" here means no gaps: every detail must lock like gears in a clock. A child lying about eating cookies might say "I didn’t touch them" but leave crumbs on their shirt. The crumbs are the inconsistency; they turn suspicion into proof.

Lies fail when facts clash. A spy claiming to be from Berlin but mispronouncing "Kurfürstendamm" (a famous street) reveals the lie—not because the truth was known, but because the error didn’t fit. Consistency isn’t just repeating the same story; it’s ensuring every piece aligns with unseen rules. Like a forger matching ink color and paper age and handwriting pressure—or the lie unravels.

Police interrogators exploit this. They ask the same question three ways: "What time did you leave?", "When did you get in your car?", "Was it dark outside?" A truthful answer stays aligned; a lie twists. Studies show liars add 30% more unnecessary details, trying to plug holes they fear exist.

Three related subjects are memory gaps in lies, how polygraphs detect stress, and why confessions cluster at night.
